speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: That's where he kept it. He lived there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They drained the pond on it?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yeah, they kicked some of it down.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: But old Summerfield was sheriff in Moscow. And Moody, Hap Moody, who's still living now, you know. I've talked to him about some of his shenanigans. Oh yeah, I know Hap Moody well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hap told me, did half ever talk to you about how he used to go in?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: No, I've never talked to him. He used to, you know, the kind of guy he was, he'd dress up like a lumberjack and that kind of thing. Well half Moody was a sheriff and Hap put somebody in jail.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: And old Jordan would take him on for a boxing match. If he was on the box, old Jordan would take him, he'd take him all on too. He'd never pass the game. That Jordan was a tough man. I thought he was kind of mean too. Oh, you bet your life he was mean and he was good too if he had to be. He was mean and he was good too. But if you weren't a boxer, old Jordan would take you on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Right there in the jail, eh? Think he used gloves?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, any old way they wanted to go at it. Well, old Jordan would take them anywhere they wanted to go.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: No, Jordan's got quite a reputation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He's dead now, isn't he?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, you keep going. Last year, he was seven. I don't think it will happen to get around to him. Well, I've seen him walking down the street in Moscow. I think half Moody is still up there. I mean, if I was a wild thing, I'd have sold him. He had an operation. He's old now. He's in his 90s, I think. He must be pretty, quite old, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was there many people that would, that would, that were informers, or would snitch if they knew that the guy was bootlegging around here?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: No. I mean, in some places, you had to keep it pretty quiet.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, you know, Jordan, they tried to keep it pretty quiet then.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I think that moonshine, well, I'm particularly moonshined to meet any more. I got some Japanese whiskey out here. It tastes just like moonshine. We brought it home from Japan just to get some bottles at the next floor 70. Still have some of it out there after six years.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: He always said it tasted just about like the old moonshine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think they brought home three bottles, wasn't it?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: We kept one, give Mark no more, one of the kids one. We went over to Expo 70 and it was the Expo 70 bottle that was probably bought at home. I think it's probably greasy, it needs to be worked on.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Better take the magic that hasn't been... Huh, well that is pretty. That's real nice. I'll latch a nice looking bottle.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: A very rare old whiskey.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: That smelled pretty strong. It must not be very good to have that much left after six years. Well, I'm just a little sad. We wanted to brew the bottle and we were over at the Expo 70.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, the one other thing that I was going to ask you about was the Depression. And how it affected people around here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it, did it hurt people pretty bad here?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh yes. People just didn't have money. When we were first married in 1929. I think it's your first job, actually after we were married, you worked with... Well, he had his own drag and he had horses and team. And did drag work and mail delivery. And then he went to work for the town as maintenance man comp, what have you. Fifty dollars a month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was that?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: About... No, it was after that. I still run the dray then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You couldn't make a living with the dray anymore?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh God, so did it. We used to run 40 to 45 carloads of coal here in the winter. Besides wood and stuff. And God told me people didn't use it anymore. They burned wood or... Different. Different than you. And began to use oil.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it have much to do with cars and trucks being around more?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I think it helped.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: It helped some. That brought in different work. That whole coal here, but the team worked until they wouldn't hardly... I'd feel ashamed of myself trying to make the hill with it. From the depot down by the river you know. Dollar a ton.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: But his first job with the town then... Well I had that. There was a comp, there was 50 dollars a month. For several years that was what it was. And that was right in the Depression. Yes, in the 30's.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well in 35 when Ken was born you quit...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you doing then when you went on to the railroad work? That was when you were doing your day work up until then, wasn't it?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: In 34. After I started home I quit the comp job here. You quit that to go down. That was in that big boy shop and these men were drowned on at Arrow. And he worked on the railroad there for a long time in the railroad. It was all washed out and no roads, nothing through here at all for several weeks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You worked to repair that road?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yes, I worked on the railroad. He worked as a watchman down at that bridge where that train went in for a long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was after the train went in?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: After the train wreck. It was after that then, it was after all three of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you hear about that? Did you hear why that wreck happened? What caused it?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Hot water.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how come they didn't know enough to stop the train?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: There was a bunch of them working this side that was there and nobody went down there. They tried to get them to go down there that little creek up and washed out the bridge. There was a little arrow that little bridge. There was a bridge out there. Howard Ghost washed out and there was no bridge there and they drove off the back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they didn't have no warning?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: No, nobody went down there that night. The train had managed to get down that far. Most of this track was all washed out and under water and everything. The train had gone that far and this water came down the other creek, that little creek. It was all washed out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they try to patrol the track in high water to check it?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: They did to a certain extent but they didn't that night. Well everybody was working I think on the other tracks and sandbagging and everybody that was available was out trying to save the rest of the track in different places. I think probably is why they weren't, didn't think about that. It wasn't so bad. It was all right. It was right at the side of it. They didn't go down there. Raleigh Albright was washed out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What part time of the day did the train go through?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: That was at night. It was at night it went through. It had always been raining. It was raining and raining and raining and raining.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the Albrights living there at that time?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh yes. There were several families of the Albrights there. That track in front of the, well where the turkey farm, Raleigh Albrights, the old folks place was up this side of the springs. That house there, Johnson's Lane was the old Albright home. That room was all washed out through there and below town and all through here so much as it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: During that depression time were there people that were, that actually gone hungry at all?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: No I don't think so. Well people raised everything then. You know they didn't go to the store and buy things like we do today. They raised their food and they canned their food and they had their cow or their chickens and you know they just depended upon their own gardens and their own cellars to live on in the winter time. Gee we never went to town and bought things and you'd make your own butter and you made your own bread and I made my own soap. And you just learned to do without things that was all. And I don't know if anybody ever went hungry because we didn't have the variety of foods that we have now. There was a place around here. Every little place around was full of people. I mean families you know lived in places that now they couldn't begin to make a living.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In town could people keep chickens and that kind of thing?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh yes. It wasn't anything that's... Oh no everybody kept a cow and chickens and maybe raised a pig and there was no restriction on what you did then. And if you didn't have it, well you just went without that was all. You just didn't have the varieties and the fresh foods and things that you have now. The money was another thing. You just didn't.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I think the first wash machine, the first electric wash machine we bought, we paid $35 for it at the old Erb Hardware in Lewiston. And we had to buy that on monthly payments to pay $35 for a wash machine. Other than that we had one of these come to me go from me kind you know that you pushed and worked. That was after we were married for a while. But money. We paid $6 rent on a house down here then. And $6 out of $50 was quite a little bit to pay for rent at that time.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: But...

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Do you think that... How long did you feel that you were scraping by before you felt you had...

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh I don't know. You were well to do. Several years. Yeah well to do.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: When... After he started on the mail job and I went back to teaching by then we began to think we had money. When I started teaching up there I think I went back for $100 a month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't it that I made teaching?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: A year and a half ago. I was stopped at $115 the last year I taught which was extra good money at that time. But no I think I had more than that up here when I started teaching. But that was in...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What 1941?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Fall of 1941 I believe. Ken was... Ken was his first year in school. So you figured that the 30s were just really lean years. The 30s were plain lean years. You bet your boots they were. They were mighty lean years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did a lot of people leave here during that time or did they hang on?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh I don't know. I think they stayed. I don't believe anybody particularly left because of that. You could have bought any place in town for a few hundred dollars if you had money to buy. You could have bought the whole town for a thousand dollars darn near. But you couldn't...

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Who'd want it?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Nobody wanted the old places you know. I mean money was so tight you couldn't pay the taxes on them. Upkeep. Why who ever thought of buying up anything then because well you just didn't have the money that was all period.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see many hobos during that time? Guys walking on the tracks?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: You used to.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: You used to. Yeah. You used to. I used to ride the blinds. Ride the blinds as I told you. There were two kids on the... riding the blinds and that went in the river down there. Two kids standing behind the coal car and the baggage car. And that went in the river that night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to them?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: They got out. It's just what the engine turned over into. The coal car turned over and the engine went down. I got a picture of that. Yeah. We looked at that. We had some pictures upstairs. Not a picture of it.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Not a picture.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do as a cop? Did you ever have to quiet people down or anything like that?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: No.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Only who was it one night that... oh West. Old Jim West.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: He was always on the rampage and his wife wanted him to go and get Jim and bring him home or something. He said well if I do I'll take him to jail. Oh no you don't put him in jail. You just leave him alone then and I'll get him. You didn't want to put him in jail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you do that? Was that something you could do as a cop? Put people in jail?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: But you never really...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: did you seriously think of leaving here and going someplace else to live?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I don't think we ever considered it. No place else was any better at that time. You didn't have money to get there if you wanted to. Well, but even when times were better, you didn't... I mean you preferred here. No. We've always had our roots here. You're employed and you're satisfied wise. And even after we retired we had no desire to leave. We've traveled all over the world a lot. It's always been nice to come back. Have a place where your roots are down. Our family has been here. I have no desire to move someplace else. Never have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know of any better growing country? Or the red?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, if we could have... our seasons are different. We don't have the rainfall that we used to. And, you know, your ground gets kind of worn out I guess. And the crops aren't as... gardens and things are harder to raise now than they used to be because you have to water them more and fertilize and spray and all of that stuff. But, as you know, a general thing, I think your climate and your conditions are pretty good here. There's really been a decrease of rain. Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Over a long period of time or just recent years?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Not over. Gradually I think for several years. There's been less rain. And during the growing season when you need it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean like the last ten years or that recent?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yes, longer than that even. Used to be these farm factors would get some tender before the bush. Now they don't get a hundred they're not satisfied. Yeah, on the farms. They're lighting everything now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the melons in the valley?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: They're awfully good. You just don't have any anymore. You might have a few in your garden. Now Arrow has them on down the canyon and they call those Giuliana melons. But they don't have them. They used to ship them out by car loads here. Truck them out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they really superior melons?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see this one picture in here of the watermelons?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Look at the size of them there. Look at the... now those were raised right over here on this hill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Forty pounds?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh yes. Those would probably all go that. But just look at them. They're great big melons.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well I think I should get going.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: They're refrigeration with an ice house. Used to buy one of those old plates they call the cables here.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: So why be that big a square packing these ice houses out here?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: That'll last until next year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Until the next winter?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was that organized? Did a bunch of guys go out together and cut or what?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: We'd take the job of putting up this service. So many tons of ice. We'd take a team of horses and sled and go over there and saw that ice out and haul her and put her... Pack it in sawdust. Pack it in sawdust.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would they use them? Would they just store fruit in their refrigeration?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh sure. She had all the refrigeration they had. After we were married we had an ice box for a long long time. Yeah, four or five years. Six years after we were married before we had our first electric freezer. Just all the night. Just ice boxes and you'd go down and get a block of ice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They would store that stuff in warehouses before shipping it sometimes for quite a while?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh no no. They'd have a building built with that white pack of sawdust. Then you'd put that in and it would all be packed. They would just take out the ice. They didn't store anything in there with the ice. They would just take it out and use it in the cafes and restaurants and the ice cream parlors. People would go down and buy a chunk of ice to put in your ice box. Lots of times you'd just put it in a tub. That's all I had for a long time. Didn't even have an ice box. Just a tub and you'd put your ice in there and put something over to hold it in there and put your milk or butter and stuff around it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did somebody own those ice houses?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh yes. They would own them. Each building would have one. The meat market had one and the stores had one. Each store would have their own ice house. They'd have their own ice house. You'd go down and buy ice from them. Very few people put it up for themselves because they didn't have a building suitable that way. They didn't get it from one of the stores. You'd go down there and buy your chunk of ice. They would just ship them cherries. They'd just ship them out as fast as they could. In the refrigerator cars that they would come and pick up the cherries. The railroad company had to keep the ice. They used ice in the cars. They'd re -ice the cars every so often. The railroad had to run the ice and everything down. The depot had an ice house. Lord, now the creek don't even freeze over anymore. Not enough water in them that you could cut a block of ice and it would be so contaminated. I suppose it was then for that matter. That's why they wouldn't have to do that now. No, you wouldn't be allowed to cut ice off of a river now and use it. That's all we ever used. Nobody died from it that I know of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a special kind of saw that they used for cutting ice? A special kind of blade?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Man saw. It's almost the same thing. Cross cut. One man saw moved down here. They used to cut them in big blocks. We used to skate from here to Kendrick on the river. In the wintertime we could skate up and down. Maybe a few places that you'd have to walk around where it's kind of riffly and rough or something. But most of the time we could skate there to Kendrick and back. That must have been fun. We did a lot of skating then. Around that way. A great deal of skating. Entertainment. A lot of fun.

speaker: Cecil Gruell
words: You don't have a fair ice tongs?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: No, I don't think we have any little ice skates anymore. You're getting a fair ice tongs. Yeah, your ice tongs. Yeah, you used to have a fair ice tongs. I don't know what happened to them. I don't know where they are. They had to dig money out of my pocket. That was about the first year we were married. That was depression years when money was money. That's all there was in was a wagon wheel. But we had to dig money up and pay for that. The banker told me to buy every horse that I could get.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Porter?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: No, Arnold Behrens.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was he telling you to do that?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, he thought it was a good investment. He thought it was going to be a big good investment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many did you get to buy?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I think four or five hundred dollars I had to dig up to pay that to come out. Which then in 1929 or 1930 was a lot of money. But after that the cherry market went bluey and people began taking out their orchards.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the end of it around the Depression?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Practically, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It had been kind of shaky before that?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, it had been real good. Our whole place here we had cherry orchard in this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the market was pretty good right up to the Depression?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: It was good until that one time when they had to pay all their freight charges. See, they sent them out on consignment. And you took what the market gave you when they got back there. You didn't have any definite price quoted you. And the market went hooey and... I don't know why they made a lot of money out of it. It was, I don't know what happened, but anyway, everybody had to dig up money to pay the freight charges. Refrigerator cars and all of that to send them back. You figured the money was made back east on the... Well, no doubt, on the jobber someplace or something. But the orchards began coming out then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they ship them back to you?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yes. Oh no, uh -uh. No, no, they just...

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: They sold low. Yes.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Not enough to pay their expenses at all. You see all their boxes and their packing, their picking and everything. They were only paying the express to me, and spending express charges on me. That was the first time that people had had to pay express. Well, that was the worst, yes. Uh -huh. That was alright. They paid $29 .30 and long food, everything was pretty darn tough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you know where they were sold back east?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: No, I don't. Not anymore. I don't know where they went. Different places at different times. I think it was in Marlboro. I know Lillian packed cherries a lot down here. I think I worked one day and I thought, no more of it. I'd rather go out and pick cherries. And I'd go out and pick cherries. And I could do better picking cherries than I could packing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the deal with packing them? Were they difficult to pack?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, no, if you got onto it. And it was tiresome just standing in that one position. And you had to pack so many cherries to a roll. And so many had to be just perfect cherries. One at a time. Yes, oh yes. And I think then we got a cent a pound for picking them. And if the picking was poor towards the end of the season or a poor tree, we'd maybe get a cent and a half or possibly two cents a pound for picking. And I could make more picking cherries than I could. We made the boxes and lidded them and loaded them into collars. Oh, sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they pack them to the box then? Do you remember?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I mean, would a box be... They were... Yes.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: And there were two sections, kind of two sections and halves. You had to have so many cherries across your first row. And the stems had to be turned up, you know, just... And then your second row had to be placed in between those stems. And every row had to be just perfect that way. I don't know whether it was two rows.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many rows to a box were there?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I've forgotten. There's a box. Probably three rows or four rows to a box. I mean, thicknesses height. About four, I think. Just pack one on top of the box. Mm -hmm. You don't see them that way in stores anymore. No, I've never seen them that way. When you open that up, the life you lived, you turned that over for a while. And your rows of cherries were just... Your stems were all under. They didn't show. They were all in the hole exactly. And it was... They were really... A good packer was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The stems didn't show up?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: No. Uh -uh. Uh -huh. And that's the way they... You had to have a stem or a cherry.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: A stemless cherry would go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the kinds that they would ship back?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Would be bings and... Bings and... Bings and raw...

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: And lambert. Lambert's mostly. All of them. Yeah, there were bings too. Bings were easier to pack. They were kind of a snub -nosed cherry. They were easier to fit in. But there weren't as many of the bings around. They would crack more easily. They sold cows to a woodlagger in Spokane. Floyd and I would take the cows and sell them to that woodlagger in Spokane. He'd come down and get a hold of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could he use them? Could he use them to make them booze?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, yeah. I didn't know that. Cherries.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, gee, I didn't think it would matter for that. Sure.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Was it making...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What you expect yours? Was it a year ago or two years ago?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Two years ago. Making... Plumb wine. Yeah, I'm pretty good. A little neighbor boy asked me what I was making and I said, yeah.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Sounds good.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Boy, he stood around for a little while and finally said, Why, I'm sure it'd take a lot of toaster that much. He was only about three years old here. Little kid across the street called Gravelle's little boy. Cute. He laughed about that boy and said, It'd take a lot of toaster that much jam.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you went picking, was that something that girls would do in groups?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, yes. Well, you were assigned, you were given a tree and you had to pick that tree clean. And you got a cent a pound for picking. Yeah, and lots of times a half or three quarters of a cent a pound if it was real good and the trees weren't too big. You had a bigger tree in the weed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which were the main orchards around here?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: A lot of people had them. Yes, yes. I used to pick a Jim Fisher's orchard a lot, which is across the bridge from out here at the end of the bridge. And, oh, there were many big orchards all around. And then the Cook Orchard up the canyon. The Cook's Orchard and Freucke's Orchard. Up the little potlatch out here. And we'd walk up there, that would be what, two or three miles at least that you'd walk up and pick cherries all day. And then walk home at night. Nobody had a car, you didn't have a car to go in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You wouldn't stay over, sleep out there?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh no, no, we'd walk back at night and walk back up in the morning and pick. But I used to pick out here at Jim Fisher's an awful lot. And then, you see, the old castle, that was known as that. The Adams Orchard, that whole place was all in orchard, a big orchard. And there were orchards all over town, all up and down every place. It was a cherry orchard for a while. And it was quite an industry here for a long time. And that was after we were married. Pull the trees out. They'd play it over here, but the old orchard was not for the old orchard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it useful enough? You make more money, did you put it in a crop after that?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, just to get the trees out of the ground when they'd gone behind them this many times. And then it got so they began to get wormy and you had to spray and do so many things that there just wasn't the money in them anymore. And people just took out their orchards to get rid of them and raise melons and tomatoes. I don't think we ever did can cherry dine. No, I don't think so. The cherries were packed out. They just didn't can them at all. They didn't can the cherries. They were packed and shipped.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, is that just because there were just too many cherries for the market?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that why they finally went out?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I think they probably got more out of them that way than canning them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I mean, in the end, it just did oversupply for them?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, I don't know. Depression years, people just didn't have the money to spend on fancy food. It was risky. If a rain came when the cherries were about ready to be harvested, then they would split and crack. And your whole crop may be just gone overnight, especially if it would rain. They were more of a flatter on the bottom of Lambert. Well, you got the pecan cherry. Well, what...

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: About like it is now in the last of June, July. The cherries are on now. That is, they're practically gone now. Right. But it would be quite a long season of picking and packing them. And then the tomatoes would be a fall crop. That was usually up in September and October, we'd worked in tomatoes. And string beans were usually later up in the... towards fall. Because they... They made it right now. They planted them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were tomatoes mostly grown that were packed down there? Were they mostly grown or right around?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: The tomatoes that were canned. Yes, were raised. Were raised around this way. And they picked them in lug boxes, the big lug box, and took them to the cannery. In big lug boxes. But they were raised here. And Mahons rented a guess a lot of the valley land down around Spalding. And I think...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: didn't they irrigate it then? Water it from the river?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I think they did. And they raised a lot of the tomatoes and beans down there that were brought up to the cannery.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are lug boxes?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Just a wooden box. About 35, 40 pounds. They weighed in about so high. And... That's about... An apple box.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Four and a half, two feet long maybe?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell
words: Well, I don't know about what we have going on here, haven't we, some?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I don't know whether we have any more or not. An apple box is taller. And the apples and pears came in a taller box and not quite so long. The lug box, the cherry boxes, and tomato boxes were longer and not quite so high. And they held about, oh, 35 to 40 pounds of cherries. It would be a lug box. Peach box, for about 30 or something. Yeah, a peach box is smaller. And these had a different bar or little handle thing across the top where your boxes now have no hand grip to them, which a lug box did. And that's what they...

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: what we used. So tomatoes were grown...

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: probably most of them didn't come down from all the way as far as Lewiston, Clark, Sierra, Mercy, from right around here. I don't know whether they brought any of the tomatoes up from Dustin's and Clarkston or not. I don't believe they did then. Here's that new beak from the University. Yeah, those tomatoes aren't doing... they have to be watered. They won't do any good. There's lots of them on, but they aren't doing so well.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, was that for work and kind of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: for work to do, was that work kind of pleasant, did you feel?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, yes. I think everybody seemed to enjoy it. They always got together, you know, and talked and visited as they worked. Oil cloth apron to keep clean. Yeah, we wore an oil cloth apron over at the cannery to keep reasonably clean. So it was kind of... it was an opportunity for community. And everybody did it, you know. Everybody was in the same boat. No one had much money and were out to make whatever they could. And it's the only work that young people could get to do. They could work in the fruit and cannery. It brought in quite a little bit of money to the town.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Do you think it...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: was it 99 % of it or 95 % of it just work, or was there really a lot of visiting that went on?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, well, it was work. You had to work or you wouldn't make anything.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: And the ones that played around, you know, just wouldn't make much. And if you were there to make money, you had to settle down the business and stay with it. And they got pretty tiresome. We sat on a lug box, these boxes turned upside down. And if you carried your own pillow, if you wanted something softer than a box to sit on, you'd take a pillow to sit on. And we sat out on an open porch, a big open part of the cannery there to work in the beans. And you sat there on that little old lug box all day long, you know, where you could beat your beans in front of you. And you put them in your lap and stem them and put them in a bucket. And it got pretty tiresome sitting there that way all day long. Tony's out there, kid. And then when we worked at Tomatoes, you stood inside and they had a belt come down with their buckets of scald tomatoes on. And then when you needed a new bucket of tomatoes, you'd take the bucket off of the belt and put your empty bucket back on. And then you'd peel them into big dish pans. They'd pour them into a big dish pan. And you'd skin them, core them, and then put them in a bucket and set it on that. I think we had something they punched each time they took a bucket. You'd punch them. But it was work, you know, any kind of automatic work, automatum that you just stayed with constantly. The same thing over and over and over and over. It was work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people take breaks fairly often?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh yes, you could. They never stopped the machinery. And, oh yeah, you could stop and have a snack and go back to work whenever you wanted to that way. There's no set time or anything when everybody quit or anything. Just on your own. That way's kind of different from like the song though where everybody had to keep working all the time. Yes. But it was work. But as I remembered, I think we had a lot of fun with it too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about, were there ever injuries that you can remember down from working in that cannery?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Nothing more than cuts. Lots of times you're working with knives all the time. And, you know, or something and you had to keep them pretty sharp. As I remember, we used to have an emery wheel that you'd go and sharpen your knife. Or your little spoon -shaped knife that you used to dig the cores out of the pears and out of the tomatoes. And you'd get cut a lot at that time that way. But outside of that, I don't think around the machinery and things. I don't remember of anyone ever being injured from it.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Anything seriously at all. I had heard that at the beginning of the cannery they had thought about it being a co -op. I thought maybe it was at the beginning, but it turned out to be private. It was private, yes.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I don't know. I think Mahon started Mahon and Dustin. But I think, I don't know whether they were together in it first and then Mahon bought Dustin out or whether Dustin came later. I think it was just Mahon for a while. I'm not sure. You could find out quite a few of the details from Catherine, though she's quite a gal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that probably most of the women in town worked at the cannery?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I think so. Everybody that could get away from home or could. A lot of them did. This picture here, this paper. I just think after a long day's work, your mother coming home and then she had to take care of the house. Well, Mother didn't work there too long. I don't believe at first. She didn't work there. As I said, my sister had to work with this other lady a lot. Her daughter worked. She had to be under her supervision for a long time until Mother did start to work. Let's see. There's one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten, eleven, twelve, thirteen women there. One, two, three, four men, fourteen women and four men and then several of us younger girls.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: That was quite a bit the crew, I believe. Mrs. Keifer, Mrs. Smith.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Mrs. McLean, that's the wrong name. That should be Mrs. McGlin. That would be Ken and John Keitens, Harry Wright, and Audis Shufir. The next two women unidentified is my sister, Lillian and Edith McGlin. Nona Reems, that should be Mona Reems. Edis Shufir, Ida Reems, Ella Brummond. The next woman unidentified is Mrs. Brummond. That's her mother, Mr. and Mrs. Hickenbottom. Gertrude Schneider, left at rear.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: That's wrong, too. Standing, sitting left at rear is Ursula McGlin. That's her sister, Mrs. McGlin. Louise Wright.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Others I unidentified. That's Minnie McGlin. That's Ursula McGlin next. There's one in back. This one in back is Gertrude Schneider. This is Ursula McGlin and Minnie McGlin and Mrs. McGlin, their mother. It looks like most of these women are either married or... Well, it's mother and daughter, an awfully lot. This is Mrs. McGlin. It says Mr. and Mrs. Hickenbottom. Well, it isn't. It's Mrs. Hickenbottom. She was a great, big, tall woman. And her daughter, Amanda, and Mrs. Brummond, and her daughter, Ella. And Mrs. Reems and her daughter, Mona Reems, is right here. This is Mrs. Shufir, or Ida Shufir. Edith McGlin, my sister, Lillian. That's Herman Shufir, Otto Shufir. Mrs. Wright, Mrs. Lillie Buchanan, Mrs. McGlin, Mr. and Mrs.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Mike Smith. Well, you know, usually, sometimes I've got the idea that most women wouldn't work or would just stay home. And maybe what it was was they didn't have the opportunity to... They think so a lot.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: They were pretty well taking care of their families at home. And as these kids got older, they worked with their mothers a lot. I think this was a smaller crew. This was more at the beginning. And then as it grew, the cannery expanded in what they can and the amounts that they can. Then more people began to work there.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, I guess what I'm thinking of, because this is one of the things that we've been trying to figure out, is like the way that women felt about work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that a lot of women would enjoy working if they could to augment the income? Did they do it only out of necessity?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Because they felt they had to do it.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Mrs. McGlin and Mrs. Lula Buchanan and Mrs. Reems were widows that had families to take care of. Mike Smith, Mr. and Mrs. Hickenbottom, their husbands, Mick Kite, several of them were widows. Then they would almost have to work. That had children and probably needed to work, yes. And I know after that, there were the Oberlanders and the McKerns that worked there an awful lot. They were both widows with children.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: And then Mrs. Bessie Buchanan was a widow and had a daughter. They worked there a lot. A great many of them, I do believe, were widows that had children. It makes me wonder what widows did when they didn't have a job. Well, as mother after, when we were left alone later on, she kept teachers an awful lot. Boarded teachers here to help support us. And did quilling and things of that kind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you make money quilting?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, no, you didn't make money. The length of time that it took to quilt a quilt, you didn't make anything. Excepting that when you got it finished, you got a little bit and returned for it. But you put in many, many, many hours quilling a quilt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who would buy a quilt?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, they didn't buy the quilt. They'd probably make the quilt and they'd hire you to do the quilting on it. Ordinary people that couldn't quilt or didn't have the time would hire you. They quilled it for a dollar a spool. You used a spool, a thread. And I don't know whether it was a hundred yards to a spool. They had much larger spools than what you have now. And they would get a dollar a spool for that thread that you'd use on the quilt. And you might use two or three spools or something on a quilt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And how long would it take?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, gracious. Days and days and days, really. Because quilling is slow and a good quiller did tiny little stitches. I got some beautiful quilts that my mother had quilled. But it took time. I used to learn to quilt. I quilled it with her many a time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it was better money instead of your work? Or what the advantages were, the canneries, compared to, say, fruit picking?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, yes. Lots of people couldn't pick fruit. You know, they just couldn't climb over a tree. It had to be a younger person that could climb around up in a tree and handle a ladder and go up and down to do that. But older people didn't pick cherries.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would the cannery run straight without going down at all?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, no. There would be breaks in it, in between different fruits and things. There used to be quite a few prunes raised around the area. And they were mostly dried. There were two or three big prune dryers up on American Ridge and around different places. Big prune orchards. But those were picked and dried in these dryers that they had. I don't think we ever fixed prunes at the cannery. But pears we did and tomatoes and beans were the main crops.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What else did they have?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Once in a while, a day or two of some other kind of fruit. But I think pears and tomatoes and beans were the main things that they can.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Do you think that, you know, in the mills, it seemed like the men working in sawmills and lumber camps were really concerned about their wages a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the women were concerned about what they were making? Do you think that, can you remember at all, were the people working there feeling that they should be getting more?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I don't think there was that feeling then. You were probably glad to get whatever they paid and would take it on grumbling, you know. It just, as a matter of life, that that's what they paid and that's what you made.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that men made considerably more than the women who worked there?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I think they did. They were paid a higher rate and they were paid by the hour. Now the overseers of the cannery, the women who were the overseers and checked on your work and so on, they were paid by the hour. But when you work by the piece, as they called it, so much a buck and so much a box, why, some people would make a great deal more than others. Because they were speedier, they could use their hands more quickly. They were married with the person as speed of engine. Yes, surely.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: It was quite an experience in our lives. But it was the only work around, you know, the fruit and vegetables at that time. That's the only work outside of the harvest fields that the men worked in the harvest and around that way. But that was the only work for women and young people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember at all on what a way it would be to make, like working on tomatoes or something like that, to make it go faster?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I mean, were there, I imagine it wouldn't take much to make a big difference in your speed. Well, you see, these tomatoes were scalded.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: And sometimes there was the hotch you could hardly put them in your hand. And you had this little knife and you had to take all of that core out. Well, if you weren't careful, you'd leave a lot of that in and maybe your bucket would come back to you when they start to pack them into the cans and out of that. And found that you were leaving skin on them or leaving some of that core in the tomato. Or if you were cutting up and wasting too much of it, by then your bucket would come back and you'd have to go through it again. So you learned to be pretty careful, you know, in coring them well. And not too deep so that you wasted a lot of the tomato and yet got that core out of them. And still had a nice, firm, solid tomato, not all broken to pieces. And then those that were broken up, they were put into different cans, cheaper class tomatoes. But you couldn't take too long. I mean, if you spent time on it, you'd be... Oh no, you bet not. You learned to be pretty fast in taking those out and going through them, you bet. And you stayed with it pretty steady, I'll tell you. But I don't remember how much a day we made.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about picking? Do you remember there how much you might make? Was pay about as good?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, of course, picking... The cherries would be at a different time from when the things at the cannery would be running. Your beans and tomatoes were later in the fall and your cherries were earlier. But you picked beans and tomatoes too. I never did. I never did. My sisters, as I said, some of them went down, they put up a tent and stayed down there and picked beans for quite a long one summer, part of a summer or something.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: And, I don't know, they were string beans, pretty good sized long string beans. I don't know what variety they were. They seemed to be a real nice bean. And they kept them coming.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, you know, the cannery and a lot of these other buildings burned down. And that's one thing I want to ask you about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you figure that some of these fires were set in Juliaetta?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, at the time everybody thoroughly believed it, yes. They were all under mysterious circumstances and business, you know, the depression years had hit and taxes and their profits were down. And it was just one way of getting out from under a white elephant.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that when the fires hit Main Street? Was depression?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: This one big store, Alexander's Store, burned the first year I taught school at Leland. That was in 1925. The fall of 1925 I started teaching the first year. And that one burned while I was up there. And then Houck's Garage, where the sundowner is now, that was a big garage that burned. One had a big hardware. Well, it was a big hardware originally, yes. A beautiful big hardware. And then it was turned into a garage. That burned in 1930. It was the day our first son was born. 1930. No, yeah, 1930. And so during that period along through there, so many of the stores and buildings burned and even houses. That was the general condition at the time, that they all burned under mysterious circumstances for no reason at all. All it was that it was insurance purposes.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: It's surprising that the town didn't burn. It didn't spread a lot more than they did. They seemed to have been...

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: You can tell with that picture by the way, but they have a look at it now. Well, let me see. What other... Well, then the cannery itself burned down. Yes, the cannery burned. I don't remember what year it burned then, a little later. And they had practically quit the cannery work when it burned. It wasn't, I don't think it had been operating recently when it burned. Well, there was suspicion that that was arsenide too. Yes, that I don't remember what year it burned. But I know they had kind of quit. It had gone, had its fling, you might say, and was through. This was after the Depression, probably.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, it was during those years, early years. I don't remember what year. Before the Depression. Well, during the 29, 30, 31. I don't know just what. Yeah, we were married in 1929, I know. And one of the buildings. Well, it was Alexander's biggest wildfires were 1920 and... Well, no. The Alexander's burned when I was teaching at Leland in 25, 26. That winter, 25 and 26, when I was teaching at Leland. And the garage burned in 1930 in June. June the 2nd, exactly, because that's when our first son was born.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you remember Alexander?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, heaven, yes. Oh,

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: yes. Uncle Sam, oh, well, Uncle Joe had a store in Lewiston. Alexander's store, a big... That was his uncle that had that place. I know of it. Yeah, old Uncle Joe. And this was young Uncle Joe, young Joe Alexander that had the store here. And I don't know whether you knew Eben Adams when he was in Moscow. I remember who he was. He was the Washington Waterpower man for a long time. He was their bookkeeper here at the store for many, many years. And clerk, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Miss Alexander local?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yes. Yeah, she was born right up here at Leland on the Fairview, just at the top of the hill, the old Johnson. And it was her sister, it was her sister's father -in -law that built the old castle, the Adams. And Mary Adams was a sister to Amanda Alexander. And they lived, Amanda lived in that house at the end of Main Street where Polumskis live now. I don't know. No, Mary lived there. Amanda lived in the next house. It was... They were well kept lovely places then. What kind of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: as a merchant, how was he?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, I think real good, yes. They had everything in their store. It was a complete department, grocery store, hard word about everything. You know, general store. It was a big store for that time. And a real good store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was he about credit?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, I don't know. I don't remember that part of it. I know everybody usually had credit. Do you think that... It's probably a lot of it. He didn't collect, probably. That would be usually for these stores. And then Niles and Needham had a store on the opposite corner, where the little city park, where that little park area is, down next to that cafe. It was Niles and Needham's store. That shows on this picture. And that was the big department store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That burned too, didn't it?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about him, Alexander, being Jewish? Do you think that any people would hold hands?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, I don't think then they did. I don't think that made any difference at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He died long before the fire burned, didn't he?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yeah, Amanda and Evan, his wife and brother -in -law practically then continued the story. But I think Joe died long before the store burned, I believe. I don't know whether Terrell is still...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, his children?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yeah, oh yeah. Ward still lives at American Falls. He's a teacher at American Falls. No, Ward did. No, not Ward. Terrell is dead. Ward's at American Falls. Yes, he lives at American Falls. He's a teacher at American Falls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Alexander's store, did they both do about the same amount of business, or was one bigger than the other? Niles and Needham burned, was out of business long before Alexander's went, wasn't it?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Anderson. Oh yeah, Anderson. People's store. People's store was known then for a while, had the store after that. And my uncle, my aunt's husband had a barber shop in that Niles and Needham store, one side of it there for a long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think people would be likely to trade at one or the other?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, I don't think so then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Trade at both?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Whatever they needed and whatever. Ah, let's see, Ruth Leland who worked at Alexander's told me that... Yes, she worked there for many, many years. She said that he got a great deal of the Indian trade out of Indians. Well, there were always quite a few of the Indians from down there. There were quite a few Indians living below town then, quite a few more than there are now. And this was their only place to come and trade, the closest place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any contacts with them? Were you meeting them?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: We knew all the Indians. We didn't have any particular contact with them. I don't think they ever came to town. The kids never went to school here. I don't remember ever any of the Indian youngsters being in school here. And I don't know where they went to school, whether they did or not. But I don't think any of them ever came to school here. No, they went to Arrow. Arrow had a school then. They did go to, they went to Arrow. There was a school there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who were some of the people that you're thinking of, the Indians that you knew?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, there was the Abel McCarthys lived across the waters. The waters. Oh. Harrison.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One of, Molly, Sevens?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yeah. Molly Sevens is in the nursing home at Lewiston. Her mother is. I see her quite often. She's dead fast most of the time. And Harrison's, oh, there were a great many of the Indians up and down the valley. But they didn't have much contact unless they'd just come to the store. I mean, they didn't, they didn't attend anything here or go to anything, as I remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, one thing that I'm curious about about them is, how do you think people felt about them when you were growing up? Did they feel that they were, you know, inferior?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, we just didn't. They were just Indians and we just didn't have any contact with them, practically. Of course, distances then were, you know, different from distances now. And I remember they used to have their sweat houses all along the river and railroad track. And we used to like to sneak over and take a look at their sweat houses along the river banks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the kids hear horror stories about Indians scalping people and staying away from them?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, not that I remember, uh -uh. I didn't know there was a lot of fear. I know Lillian used to say that she used to be scared of the Indians that sit along the main street or come up and sit around the street. But I don't remember ever being afraid of them or anything. Yeah. I guess there were places in Lewiston where they couldn't go in. Oh, yes. Oh, yes. They couldn't even enter. They couldn't go into the cafes and places. Or even... You couldn't sell beer. Oh, no, you couldn't sell beer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Even in town here or something like that?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Any place. The only person I remember ever being afraid of was an old shoemaker. He had a little shop across the street from, well, next to the old hotel. A little old two -story ramshack of the old building. But he was a shoe cobbler. We'd have to take our shoes down to be half sold. And I was particularly hard on shoes. And so I had to have my shoes half sold or the toes fixed about every so often. Of course, high shoes, you know. Oh, he used to be scared to death. He was, I think, Austrian or Hungarian Austrian probably. And he couldn't speak English. And he had a foul -smelling pipe and he always had that stump of that old pipe in his mouth. And I would take my shoes down and get my shoes or take shoes down to him to be fixed. And I know one time when I took my shoes down, he gave me a nickel. And boy, after that, he was a pretty good guy. I wasn't afraid of him anymore because he couldn't talk and he was crippled. And everybody said, oh, music will get you. I'll send you down to old music. He was just kind of the boogeyman around town. Never married or anything. Had no family. Just lived there alone and existed alone. Pretty good old shoemaker. Probably scared the kids probably. Yes, and because he couldn't talk, you know, English and then mother and just a few words of being crippled that way. Oh, I used to be scared to death. Music. M -O -Z -I -K. No, there was a K. Z in it. Music. I don't know what his first name was. It was just music as far as I remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the children's day at the park?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yes.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I have a picture of while out here on the yard, the winding the maypole. We always had maypole winding on children's day. And we'd wind the maypoles, put up a tall pole and have your streamers, you know, march around and weave in around, you know, weave that pole with the long streamers, mostly paper. And everybody dressed up in their finest white dresses. We've got a picture of the group winding the maypole out here in our yard one year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this an extensive affair? Did it attract people?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I don't know whether that was, I think that was through the Sunday school. I think the one that we had out here in the yard, I think, was a Sunday school children's day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was right here in the hole?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yes, in our yard out here. That particular winding the maypole was. We got a picture and didn't have too many pictures of things in those days. But it's a pretty good sized picture of all the kids winding the maypole.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So was this an annual event?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I think it was, quite so. We used to have it quite often. Because I had heard that they had it often down at the park. Down where the cannery, in that area where the mill is now, was the park area. And it was always quite a, quite a celebration quite a day. They used to have the Chautauqua tents come and put up a big Chautauqua tent and have their entertainments there, you know, caddling groups would come through for that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it popular in Chautauqua?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh yes, yes that was looked forward to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about it? What they did?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, I don't know. Usually they would have, oh maybe little plays and magicians and, oh I don't know, kind of almost on a circus type almost that would be. But I don't remember too much of the programs that they had excepting that they would have these Chautauqua tents come through. And people would attend from all around the place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the park get a lot of use by the town when the park was there?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: It was the only ball field around and they had a big ball field there and they had lots of ball games. And we always had our school picnics and the Fourth of July celebrations were usually held there at the park, a large part of them. And up and down Main Street there's speakers and things that I think it was used quite a bit, yes. And only kids would go there to play.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Because it does seem to me, it's something I remember you mentioning at that time.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: When I thought about it, it certainly seems like, although I never saw it then, it must have been a really nice place. There were the big cottonwood trees and evergreen trees, but a lot of big cottonwood trees all around through it. And around Blue Spring. It was a nice place, I wish we had it now. First the mill has brought in a lot of money and a lot of work to people that the park of course didn't. It's too bad, but it was the only area suitable for a park and we lost it. It made me wonder if there wasn't any place else they could have put the mill. But you know, your hindsight is always better than your foresight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was it that the mill came here, more or less? Was it in the 50s? Oh golly, let me see, 20 years?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: It must have been.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did the Kirkpatrick start, at the mill?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
Sam Schrager
words: 40s, was it that far? Has it been 30 years that it's been going? 35 years?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I don't know whether it's been that long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 50s? Do you think at the time, I mean at the time, did you have misgivings about it?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: It would be doing well at the time when it was built. They figured 10 years. And it's been far longer than 10 years. And it has increased and been rebuilt and fixed over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the city deed the park to them? For what was it, a dollar a year?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: A dollar that they deeded, gave them the ground for a dollar a year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did it get more than that?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I don't know. That was the beginning, I think it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your family active in any of the churches?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yes, Mother, this is, they said the dugout, as it was called, the dugout, the basement church, was a Methodist church. And she and the family worked in that Methodist church until the top of it was built.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have money to build the top?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: No, at first. It was just the basement part. They had services in the basement in that part of it until they could get money enough to build the top. And that was built on then. So we've always, it's been our church, we've always been there in that particular church with my family.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I got the impression that Juliaettata has had many churches and it's been a very strong church community. Too many churches for a small area. You see this Lutheran church was right down here, the second house below here, just in back of this one on the corner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And it burned, was there a Christmas program?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: After a Christmas program, I believe, at Christmas time, whether candles or, I don't remember, an overheated stove. And it burned. And then they built the new Lutheran church on the street above Ted Grosgos' there on that nice brick building. And that's where the old Christian church used to be, that was torn down, and this was built. And then there was a Catholic church,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: well, straight across from the school, where, what's the other Grosgos that lives over there, the yellow house?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Ted, Janice Grosgos' husband. Where they are, it's across the street from that, there was a Catholic church. I think that, no, just below them. It was torn down, it didn't burn, it was torn down, and the Catholics were never very strong here. At that time there were quite a few, and when it just disbanded, that was torn down. And then there was the old Presbyterian church that has been made over into a home where Skaggs live. I don't know if you know their house. That was the old Presbyterian church, and that was remodeled into a home. What do you, um, you know, I've seen this in other towns, too, and that there are kind of too many churches. Too many churches for a small community to support. Like the Christian church died, too. Yes, and it was, as you can see by the picture, that that was a big, big church, a big thriving church at one time there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think would be the cause or the reason why, say, a church like the Christian church would decline?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: At that time there weren't too many of the churches here. I think the Christian and the, uh, Christian church and the Presbyterian church, we worked in the Presbyterian church, I know, when we first came here. That was Mother's church, until it disbanded and then the Southermen started. And the Presbyterian and the Christian and the Lutheran churches, I believe, were the three particularly strong churches then. You see, now we have the Nazarene and the Seventh -day Adventists combined with the Mormon. The Seventh -day Adventists have it on Saturdays, and the Mormons have started recently now just within the last year. It's the first time there's ever been a Mormon church here. And that started, and they have the Seventh -day Adventist church on Sunday, the Adventists have it on Saturday. And they're picking up quite a few now, I understand. And then there is the community church, the Methodist church, disbanded as a Methodist church. Oh, the Methodist church did. Uh -huh, several years ago, when it, uh, all conference claims and expenses, you had to send so much money out to maintain it in the conference. And then Kendrick and Juliaettata were combined, and Leland in the Methodist unity church, which meant that you only had services divided. Divided. Well, the minister went first one place and then the other, and he didn't live here and didn't give much time. And so we became just a community church, an independent community church, and that's what we have now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that, is that enlarged membership?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: No, it hasn't. Some of them retained their membership in the Methodist church at Kendrick when it disbanded. We had our choice of putting our membership in the Kendrick Methodist church or leaving it as a community church here. And then the Lutheran church is quite strong here. But when you take that many active churches, for a small community, it divides.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think happened to the Presbyterian church?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I don't remember what, uh, what caused the Presbyterian church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: People?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, it became a united brethren. Yes, the united brethren church took over the Presbyterian church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With some of the same members?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yes, uh -huh. And that's, I think, when we went into the Methodist church then, the united brethren. They were quite strong here for quite a long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then it gradually, I guess, just quit, didn't they?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: They just, they just, for some reason, I guess most of them either moved away or older or farther, and it just quit. And then that was when the building was built by, was bought by Art Johns, and he made it over into this home then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the people's church affiliation has been decided a lot by their, by, you know, where their friends are going to church?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh yes, I think so.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I think that has a lot to do with it. Now, there's quite a large number of the younger group that have gone into the Lutheran church because they're quite, well, demanding in their membership. And those that were Lutherans, you know, have stayed with it, and it has brought in, I think, quite a few more of the younger generation, I believe. You're going to say demanding. Well, that, well, they demand your attendance, you might say, and support a great deal more than some of the congregations do. And their confirmations and classes that they have for their children and so on. And there are quite a few of the younger, young married group that are quite good workers in the Lutheran church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have revivals been important to Juliaetta?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yes, I should say in the early days revivals were, you might say, the highlight of the winter. When a revival came to town, everybody went to church. And especially the early Methodist church and the United Brethren churches, they had some rip -roars. Maybe they would have a couple of weeks or so at a time, first going to church and the other, and they were really...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they be very successful as far as conversion?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, yes, for the time being, at least, how long they stayed with it, I don't remember. And of course some of them did, but then, oh, you bet. And they would have their baptisms down at the river, and yes, it was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what the preaching was like?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well, health, harm, and still mostly, I think, and pretty loud and all. And you bet, if you didn't do as I say, you'd sure go to hell in a hurry. I would think that that would be, you know, as I think, as a kid... Well, it worked on the emotions of people, you know. That was it, it was emotional, and people made a big to -do over it, and a big rip -roaring and yelling and shouting and amen -ing, you know. It was just more emotional than religious, actually.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the young people? Would this be something they'd get involved in, too?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, yes, definitely. I would think as a kid I'd be scared by that kind of thing myself. Well, of course, I don't know, I don't think anyone has really had that... I don't remember anybody ever feeling that way about it, excepting that, of course, you know, well, my friends went forward and did all this, but then, of course, I should, too, you know, and it was a chain event a lot. A chain reaction. Rather than actual religious motives, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Skin deep, huh?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Skin deep. Yes, skin deep. It sounds to me like you'd almost feel, you could feel pressure, too, if everyone was just... Well, there was, there was pressure, yes. A great deal, oh boy, sometimes those services, the last half of the night, you know, and really...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would kids stay up like that?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: And then everybody took all the youngsters to church. I mean, you know, you didn't have a babysitter downstairs for all the kids to go to. They went with their parents and stayed with their parents during the church. If they went to sleep, I stretched down the bench and went to sleep. But the families as a whole always attempted to get...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much socializing in that, in the revival, too, or was it mostly just the religious?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Well...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did, for instance, would they bring dinners and, I mean, have potlucks and that?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: No, I don't think so. I don't remember of that. I don't think so. It was just... Well, that would cut it down, that socializing part of it. It was just playing church services, get along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It would be in the church?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Oh, yes. In the winter, it would have to be. Yes, uh -huh. And that's when they usually were, in the winter. Yes, I think so. A great deal more in the wintertime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that would affect the church membership as far as what church you would belong to? Would that realign people's affiliations?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: I imagine it did to a certain extent. It did. I know the, well, I think the Presbyterians had, too, had their revival meetings. I think all the earlier churches did ever so often. All denominations. Yes, I think they all did. And usually had outside speakers, you know, preachers come through that were just evangelists.

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: But somehow, when I think of the Presbyterians, although I don't know much about the early religion there, it seems like they would be more, they would be less demonstrative of some of the...

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: Yes, I think so. And the Christian church, as a rule, wasn't so much either, I don't believe. I know we used to, there used to be before we even started, I know we went, but I think before we even became members of the Methodist church in the dugout at the lower park, boy, they had some rip -roars down there going a lot of times. And I know they used to have conference, the church conference quite a little bit, and that was when quite a few of the Indians from Lapley and Spalding always came up to the conference meetings. They attended quite often here. And that was, I think, after the top of the church was built, and Haskell was...

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell
words: That was about the time we were married, wasn't it?

speaker: Crystal Ottosen Gruell or Cecil Gruell
words: It was built before we were married. Haskell was here, and he married us, and he had married my brother. And that was the first wedding he had had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was Alton G .D., when was Alton Hazel married?
